planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; riberry is an Australian native rainforest tree producing abundant, small (1/2-inch), vivid crimson, pear-shaped fruits with a sour-sweet, spicy flavor combining apple and clove-cinnamon notes — widely used in Australian native food products and highly regarded as a bushfood ingredient.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; riberry can be pruned as a formal hedge or left to grow as a specimen small tree. Syzygium luehmannii (riberry or small-leaf lillypilly) is an Australian native rainforest tree from the subtropical and warm-temperate rainforests of New South Wales and Queensland — one of the most commercially important of the Australian lilly pilly (Syzygium) species in the native food industry. The tree produces abundant clusters of small (1/2-inch), teardrop-shaped, vivid magenta-crimson fruits, and is one of the most prolific and reliable fruiting lilly pillies in the genus. The riberry flavor is unique and highly prized: sour, fruity, and sweet with distinctive warm cinnamon-clove spice notes from the high eugenol content — a flavor combination with no equivalent in European or American fruits. In Australian native food (bushfood) cuisine, riberries are used in jams, sauces, wines, ice creams, vinegars, and as a garnish for savory dishes — particularly with game, lamb, and cheese. As a landscape plant, riberry is compact, attractive, and responds very well to formal clipping into hedge shapes; the coppery-red new growth flushes make it ornamentally outstanding. Its combination of ornamental and food value makes it one of the best lilly pillies for zone 9–12 edible landscapes.
sources: Royal Botanic Garden Sydney — Syzygium luehmannii

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, coastal soil in full sun to part shade; muntries is a low-growing, ground-hugging Australian native groundcover shrub from the coastal heathlands and mallee scrub of South Australia and western Victoria — producing small, round, green to purple-red berries with an apple-spice flavor four times higher in antioxidants than blueberries by weight.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly, coastal-type soil in full sun to part shade; drought-tolerant once established; avoid clay or poorly drained soils. Kunzea pomifera (muntries, emu apple, or native cranberry) is a prostrate, ground-hugging Australian native shrub from the coastal heathlands, mallee scrub, and sand dunes of South Australia and western Victoria — one of the most remarkable and underappreciated Australian native food plants. The plant spreads slowly as a low (6–18 inch), dense, aromatic groundcover with tiny, dark-green leaves and small white bottlebrush-like flowers, eventually producing clusters of small (1/4–1/2 inch), round berries that ripen from green to red-purple in late summer. The berries have a unique, complex, sweet-spicy flavor combining apple, cinnamon, and a subtle eucalyptus note — quite unlike any other berry. Nutritionally, muntries are exceptional: ORAC (antioxidant) values four times higher than blueberries by weight, with high vitamin C, flavonoids, and polyphenols. Aboriginal Australians historically ate muntries extensively as a major food source across the southern Australian coast. In the modern Australian native food industry, muntries are used in jams, sauces, ice creams, and gourmet berry products, and are increasingly available from specialty nurseries. As a garden plant, muntries serves as an excellent low-maintenance native groundcover that also produces an edible crop.
sources: Australian Native Food Industry — Kunzea pomifera Factsheet
Royal Botanic Gardens Victoria — Kunzea pomifera

planting.note: Plant grafted container stock in spring in well-drained, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; grafted trees produce in 4–6 years vs. 8–12 from seed; macadamia is native to the subtropical rainforests of southeast Queensland and northeast New South Wales — the only major commercial nut crop to originate from Australia — producing the world's most expensive commercial nut, with creamy, buttery, sweet, incomparably rich flavor.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; protect from frost below 26°F until established; plant 2+ varieties for cross-pollination and better yields. Macadamia integrifolia (smooth-shell macadamia) is one of the world's most celebrated nut trees and the only major food crop originating in Australia — native to the subtropical rainforests of southeast Queensland. The macadamia nut is universally recognized as one of the finest-flavored nuts in the world: round, ivory-white, buttery-smooth, with an extraordinarily rich, creamy, sweet flavor unlike any other nut. The fat content (75%) is predominantly monounsaturated, making macadamia oil one of the most desirable culinary and cosmetic plant oils. The smooth-shell species (M. integrifolia) is the primary commercial macadamia — most Hawaiian, Australian, South African, and Kenyan commercial orchards are primarily or entirely M. integrifolia or its hybrids. Trees are evergreen, grow slowly to 25–35 feet, produce dark-green, glossy, leathery leaves, and begin bearing in 5–7 years from grafted stock. The extremely hard shell (requiring 300+ psi to crack) requires a specialized macadamia cracker. Zone 9b–11, damaged below 26°F. Hawaii, California, and South Florida can grow macadamia commercially; zone 9a gardens can succeed with protected sites.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Macadamia Production
Queensland Department of Agriculture — Macadamia Industry

planting.note: Plant grafted container stock in spring in well-drained, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; rough-shell macadamia (M. tetraphylla) is marginally more cold-hardy than smooth-shell M. integrifolia, tolerating brief dips to about 24–26°F in established trees — slightly extending the zone range into protected zone 8b–9a sites; the prickly-edged leaves (unlike the smooth-edged M. integrifolia) distinguish the species.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; M. tetraphylla is slightly more cold-tolerant than M. integrifolia and may succeed in protected zone 8b sites. Macadamia tetraphylla (rough-shell macadamia or prickly-leaf macadamia) is the second of the two commercially important macadamia species — closely related to the smooth-shell macadamia (M. integrifolia) but distinguishable by its prickly-edged leaves arranged in whorls of four (the species name 'tetraphylla' means 'four-leaved'), and by the rough, ridged texture of its nut shell. Both species produce equally delicious, buttery, rich macadamia nuts with the same world-class flavor, and in commercial macadamia production, the two species and their hybrids are grown side by side with little practical flavor difference noted by consumers. The practical distinction for home growers is cold tolerance: M. tetraphylla is marginally more cold-hardy than M. integrifolia, typically tolerating temperatures of 24–26°F in established trees — potentially extending the range to protected zone 8b–9a microclimates in warm-wall or frost-pocket-free sites. M. tetraphylla is also slightly smaller in tree size. Both species benefit from cross-pollination, and planting one of each species is commonly recommended to ensure good nut set.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Macadamia Species
California Rare Fruit Growers — Macadamia tetraphylla
Queensland Department of Agriculture — Macadamia Species Comparison

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Ethiopian moringa (M. stenopetala) is the 'other moringa' — a species native to southern Ethiopia and northern Kenya that produces the same edible large leaves, pods, and seeds as common moringa (M. oleifera) but with larger, softer, milder-flavored leaves eaten as a staple vegetable across the Konso region of Ethiopia, where it has been cultivated as a major food crop for centuries longer than M. oleifera.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; coppice every few months to maintain leaf production. Moringa stenopetala (Ethiopian moringa or cabbage tree) is the second commercially important moringa species after M. oleifera — native to the semi-arid highland areas of southern Ethiopia and northern Kenya, particularly the Konso region of the Omo Valley, where it has been cultivated and consumed as a major vegetable crop for an estimated 2,000+ years, far longer than M. oleifera in most of Africa. The tree is sometimes called 'cabbage tree' because the large, oval, blue-green leaves (much larger than M. oleifera's tiny leaflets) are eaten cooked in large quantities like cabbage — chopped and boiled or sautéed as a staple vegetable, often cooked with sorghum paste (injera dough), and also added to bean stews. The flavor is milder and less bitter than M. oleifera leaves. Like its more famous relative, M. stenopetala has exceptional nutritional density — high in protein, iron, calcium, vitamins A and C. The seeds produce a high-quality oil (ben oil) used for cooking. M. stenopetala is more adapted to cooler, higher-elevation tropical conditions than M. oleifera, and may perform better in humid tropical highland climates. Both can be grown in zone 10–12 lowland tropical gardens.
sources: FAO — Moringa stenopetala: A Lesser-Known Species
International Centre for Research in Agroforestry (ICRAF)
Konso Cultural Landscape UNESCO Documentation — Traditional Agriculture

subcategory: Mediterranean wild fruit and spice tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or gravelly, limestone-derived soil in full sun; terebinth is an ancient Mediterranean deciduous shrub or small tree — the wild ancestor of the cultivated pistachio (P. vera) — producing small, olive-shaped berries that dry to a deep red-brown; widely used in Turkish cuisine as 'menengiç' for a traditional coffee-substitute beverage, and as a spice throughout the Middle East.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or limestone-derived soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant once established — a true Mediterranean xerophyte. Pistacia terebinthus (terebinth or turpentine tree) is an ancient wild Mediterranean tree in the cashew family — the wild relative of the commercial pistachio (P. vera) — native to the dry hillsides, maquis, and garrigue scrublands of the Mediterranean basin from Portugal and Morocco east through Turkey and the Levant. The terebinth is one of the most historically significant trees of the ancient world: it appears in the Hebrew Bible (the 'elah' of the story of David and Goliath), in Greek mythology, and throughout classical Mediterranean literature. It produces small (1/4-inch), olive-shaped drupes that dry from red to dark brown-purple — the 'terebinth berries' or 'menengiç' used in Turkish cuisine as a traditional spice and, most distinctively, as a caffeine-free coffee substitute: roasted and ground terebinth berries are brewed exactly like coffee (menengiç kahvesi — terebinth coffee), producing a thick, rich, slightly bitter, nutty, resinous beverage beloved in southeastern Turkey and the Levant. The tree also produces a historical resin (Chian turpentine) used in medicine and varnishes in antiquity. As a garden plant, terebinth is an attractive, hardy, drought-tolerant Mediterranean small tree or large shrub with graceful pinnate leaves turning brilliant red-orange in autumn.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Mediterranean Shrubs
